writing_type: policy advocacy and public comment writing
user_goals_summary: This user writes policy arguments for mixed audiences — regulators, journalists, and advocates — and values writing that earns the reader's attention through human experience before asserting institutional arguments. Their highest priority is accessible precision: every technical term must be immediately grounded in concrete, experiential language so that sophistication never becomes a barrier to comprehension, especially at the moments where the text makes its key asks.

rubric.name: Accessible Precision
rubric.description: Every technical, specialist, or insider term is immediately tethered to a concrete explanation — an example, a description of experience, or a plain-language clause that shows the reader what the term means in practice. The goal is to name it, then immediately show it, so the reader never has to translate on the fly.

dimensions.label: No jargon or term of art appears without an immediate plain-language tether
Explanations use examples or descriptions of experience rather than formal definitions
Reader understanding is most carefully supported at the moments where the text makes its key claims or requests
Technical concepts retain their substantive precision even after being made accessible — nothing is dumbed down

rubric.name: Audience-First Framing
rubric.description: The writing leads with human experience and felt stakes, not institutional framing or procedural context. Regulatory and organizational context earns its place only after the reader already cares. The register works simultaneously for expert and non-expert audiences without condescending to either.

dimensions.label: The opening leads with a human experience or felt problem, not institutional framing
The reader understands why they should care before being asked to follow the argument's architecture
The tone works for both expert readers and sophisticated non-experts without condescending
Regulatory, legal, or organizational context appears only after the reader has a reason to engage with it

rubric.name: Claim-Before-Evidence Structure
rubric.description: Each argumentative section leads with the principle or assertion it exists to establish, then marshals evidence in support. A skimming reader who reads only the first sentence of each paragraph can reconstruct the core argument without the details.

dimensions.label: Each argumentative paragraph or section opens with the claim or principle it exists to establish
Research, examples, and data appear as support after the governing assertion, not before it
A reader who reads only topic sentences can reconstruct the core argument
Each section's final sentence articulates why the evidence matters, not just what it shows

rubric.name: Strategic Pragmatism in Recommendations
rubric.description: Recommendations demonstrate implementation thinking, not just ideals. The writing offers both the full vision and a meaningful fallback, so the decision-maker has something to land on even if the maximalist ask is too much. Each tier is framed as independently valuable.

dimensions.label: Recommendations include both an ideal outcome and a narrower but still meaningful fallback
Asks demonstrate understanding of practical constraints on the decision-maker
Each level of recommendation is presented as independently valuable, not as a reluctant compromise
Recommendations identify concrete mechanisms for how the proposed action would work, not just desired outcomes

rubric.name: Evidence-Based Authority
rubric.description: Claims are grounded in named research, documented practices, or concrete examples — not assertion or emotional appeal. The tone conveys expertise and seriousness. Every piece of evidence is visibly connected to the claim it supports.

dimensions.label: Claims are grounded in named research, institutions, or documented practices
Evidence includes specific, recognizable examples rather than abstract descriptions
Each piece of evidence is clearly connected to the claim it supports
The register conveys expertise and seriousness rather than frustration, complaint, or venting

rubric.name: Skimmability and Visual Structure
rubric.description: The document respects that not every reader will read start to finish. Section headers function as signposts that tell a skimmer what each block is doing — plain, declarative phrases, not formal legal section titles. A reader can enter at any section and orient themselves.

dimensions.label: Section headers signal what each block is doing, not just what it's about
Headers read as declarative signposts, not formal court-brief section titles
A reader who enters at any section can orient themselves without having read from the beginning
The relationship between sections is apparent from headers and opening sentences alone

coaching_notes: This user's single most persistent concern is jargon that isn't immediately grounded — they will flag orphaned technical terms repeatedly until each one is tethered to a concrete explanation. They think structurally about paragraph function and expect each block to have a clear, skimmable role. They are pragmatic about policy audiences: they want to show decision-makers they've thought about implementation, not just stated ideals. When giving feedback, they articulate the underlying principle they want followed ('name it, then show it'), which makes their preferences highly transferable across revisions.

writing_type: policy advocacy and public comment writing
user_goals_summary: This user writes policy arguments for mixed audiences — regulators, journalists, and advocates — and values writing that earns the reader's attention through human experience before asserting institutional arguments. Their highest priority is accessible precision: every technical term must be immediately grounded in concrete, experiential language so that sophistication never becomes a barrier to comprehension, especially at the moments where the text makes its key asks.
coaching_notes: This user's single most persistent concern is jargon that isn't immediately grounded — they will flag orphaned technical terms repeatedly until each one is tethered to a concrete explanation. They are pragmatic about policy audiences: they want to show decision-makers they've thought about implementation, not just stated ideals. writing_type: public regulatory comments and policy advocacy
user_goals_summary: The user writes public comments addressed to federal regulatory agencies, designed for a mixed audience of agency staff, political appointees, journalists, and advocates. They value writing that leads with human stakes, grounds claims in named evidence, makes technical and legal concepts immediately accessible without dumbing them down, and offers policymakers tiered recommendations that demonstrate implementation thinking. The register should convey earned authority through evidence and reasoning — never through emotional appeal, performative rhetoric, or insider jargon that excludes non-specialist readers.

rubric.name: Audience-First Framing
rubric.description: The writing leads with human experience and felt stakes, not institutional framing or procedural context. Regulatory and organizational context earns its place only after the reader already cares. Human stakes introduced early persist throughout the document — the reader never loses contact with the people affected, even in the most technical or legal sections.

dimensions.label: The opening leads with a human experience or felt problem, not institutional framing
The reader understands why they should care before being asked to follow the argument's architecture
The tone works for both expert readers and sophisticated non-experts without condescending
Regulatory, legal, or organizational context appears only after the reader has a reason to engage with it
Human stakes introduced early remain present or are reconnected in later sections — the affected person never fully disappears from the argument

rubric.name: Accessible Precision
rubric.description: Every technical, specialist, or insider term is immediately tethered to a concrete explanation — an example, a description of experience, or a plain-language clause that shows the reader what the term means in practice. The goal is to name it, then immediately show it, so the reader never has to translate on the fly. This is most critical at the moments where the text makes its key claims or asks.

dimensions.label: No jargon or term of art appears without an immediate plain-language tether
Explanations use examples or descriptions of experience rather than formal definitions
Reader understanding is most carefully supported at the moments where the text makes its key claims or requests
Technical concepts retain their substantive precision even after being made accessible — nothing is dumbed down

rubric.name: Claim-Before-Evidence Structure
rubric.description: Each argumentative section leads with the principle or assertion it exists to establish, then marshals evidence in support. A skimming reader who reads only the first sentence of each paragraph can reconstruct the core argument. Each section completes its own argumentative arc — diagnosis lands on prescription — so that no subsequent section is left carrying double duty.

dimensions.label: Each argumentative paragraph or section opens with the claim or principle it exists to establish
Research, examples, and data appear as support after the governing assertion, not before it
A reader who reads only topic sentences can reconstruct the core argument
Each section finishes its own argument with a conclusion or prescription — no section ends on diagnosis alone, leaving subsequent sections overloaded
Each section's final sentence articulates why the evidence matters or what follows from it, not just what it shows

rubric.name: Strategic Pragmatism in Recommendations
rubric.description: Recommendations demonstrate implementation thinking, not just ideals. The writing offers both the full vision and a meaningful fallback, so the decision-maker has something to land on even if the maximalist ask is too much. Each tier is framed as independently valuable, not as a reluctant compromise.

dimensions.label: Recommendations include both an ideal outcome and a narrower but still meaningful fallback
Asks demonstrate understanding of practical constraints on the decision-maker
Each level of recommendation is presented as independently valuable, not as a reluctant compromise
Recommendations identify concrete mechanisms for how the proposed action would work, not just desired outcomes

rubric.name: Evidence-Based Authority
rubric.description: Claims are grounded in named research, documented practices, or concrete examples — not assertion or emotional appeal. The tone conveys earned authority through the strength of the evidence and reasoning, not through rhetorical performance. Closing and summative sentences carry gravity without triumphalism — the reader should feel the weight of the record, not the confidence of the advocate.

dimensions.label: Claims are grounded in named research, institutions, or documented practices
Evidence includes specific, recognizable examples rather than abstract descriptions
Each piece of evidence is clearly connected to the claim it supports
The overall register conveys expertise through reasoning and evidence rather than emotional appeal or rhetorical flourish
Summative and closing sentences convey the weight of the record rather than performing the advocate's certainty — the reader is shown the reasoning, not told the answer

rubric.name: Skimmability and Visual Structure
rubric.description: The document respects that not every reader will read start to finish. Section headers function as declarative signposts that tell a skimmer what each block is doing and where the argument lands — not formal legal section titles. A reader can enter at any section and orient themselves.

dimensions.label: Section headers signal what each block is arguing or concluding, not just what it's about
Headers read as declarative signposts, not formal court-brief section titles
A reader who enters at any section can orient themselves without having read from the beginning
The relationship between sections is apparent from headers and opening sentences alone

coaching_notes: Non-negotiable: every technical term must be immediately grounded — this user will revise repeatedly until jargon is tethered to experience. They think structurally about their audience: different readers enter at different points, and the document must work for all of them. They strongly prefer showing the reader the reasoning over declaring the conclusion; any sentence that sounds like it's performing confidence rather than demonstrating it will get flagged. Recommendations should always be tiered — this user views maximalist-only asks as strategically naive. Human stakes must persist throughout the document, not just open it; if a person is introduced in the opening, they should be felt in the recommendation.

rubric.name: Strategic Pragmatism in Recommendations

Asks demonstrate understanding of practical constraints on the decision-maker

Recommendations identify concrete mechanisms for how the proposed action would work, including existing infrastructure or precedent that supports feasibility

rubric.name: Evidence-Based Authority
rubric.description: Claims are grounded in named research, documented practices, or concrete examples — not assertion or emotional appeal. Evidence does argumentative work, not just evidentiary work: the reader can follow the inferential chain from data to the specific conclusion drawn, rather than seeing evidence and conclusions running in parallel. The tone is firm but fair — opposition is understood before it is rejected, and summative sentences carry the weight of the record rather than performing the advocate's certainty.

dimensions.label: Claims are grounded in named research, institutions, or documented practices
Evidence includes specific, recognizable examples rather than abstract descriptions
Evidence carries the inferential chain — the reader can follow why the data leads to the specific conclusion drawn, rather than seeing evidence and argument as parallel tracks
Opposing positions or industry objections are characterized fairly and addressed on their strongest terms — the argument is firm without being adversarial

The overall register conveys expertise through reasoning rather than rhetorical performance, and summative sentences carry gravity without triumphalism — the reader feels the weight of evidence, not the confidence of the advocate

rubric.name: Skimmability and Visual Structure
rubric.description: The document respects that not every reader will read start to finish. Section headers function as declarative signposts that tell a skimmer what each block is doing and where the argument lands — not formal legal section titles. A reader can enter at any section and orient themselves.

dimensions.label: Section headers signal what each block is arguing or concluding, not just what it's about
Headers read as declarative signposts, not formal court-brief section titles
A reader who enters at any section can orient themselves without having read from the beginning
The relationship between sections is apparent from headers and opening sentences alone

coaching_notes: Non-negotiables: experiential opening before any regulatory framing, tiered recommendations in every piece, and no orphaned jargon — especially at the point of the ask. This user's instinct when something feels wrong is almost always structural (evidence and conclusions not integrated, sections not completing their arcs) rather than sentence-level. They value charitable framing of opposition as a credibility signal and will flag language that sounds adversarial or triumphalist; their preferred register is 'firm but fair, urgent without being alarmist.' When offering options, lean toward the one that puts the reader inside the experience rather than observing it.

rubric.name: Claim-Before-Evidence Structure
rubric.description: Each argumentative section leads with the principle or assertion it exists to establish, then marshals evidence in support. A skimming reader who reads only the first sentence of each paragraph can reconstruct the core argument. Each section completes its own argumentative arc — diagnosis lands on prescription — and the inferential bridge between the specific problem identified and the specific remedy proposed is made explicit.

dimensions.label: Each argumentative paragraph or section opens with the claim or principle it exists to establish
Research, examples, and data appear as support after the governing assertion, not before it
A reader who reads only topic sentences can reconstruct the core argument
Each section finishes its own argument with a conclusion or prescription — no section ends on diagnosis alone, leaving subsequent sections overloaded
When a section diagnoses a problem and leads to a specific remedy, the text makes explicit why this particular instrument follows from this particular diagnosis rather than an alternative

rubric.name: Strategic Pragmatism in Recommendations
rubric.description: Recommendations demonstrate implementation thinking, not just ideals. The writing offers both the full vision and a meaningful fallback, so the decision-maker has something to land on even if the maximalist ask is too much. Each tier is framed as independently valuable, not as a reluctant compromise. Recommendations are specific enough that a drafter could use them.

dimensions.label: Recommendations include both an ideal outcome and a narrower but still meaningful fallback
Asks demonstrate understanding of practical constraints on the decision-maker
Each level of recommendation is presented as independently valuable, not as a reluctant compromise
Recommendations identify concrete mechanisms for how the proposed action would work, including existing infrastructure or precedent that supports feasibility
Recommendations are specific enough that a staff attorney or rule drafter could point to them as a basis for rule text

rubric.name: Evidence-Based Authority
rubric.description: Claims are grounded in named research, documented practices, or concrete examples — not assertion or emotional appeal. Evidence does argumentative work, not just evidentiary work: it builds to the conclusion rather than sitting beside it, so the reader follows the inferential chain from data to the specific conclusion drawn. The tone is firm but fair, and summative sentences carry the weight of the record rather than performing the advocate's certainty.

dimensions.label: Claims are grounded in named research, institutions, or documented practices
Abstract or structural claims are grounded in specific, recognizable individual-level examples or transaction sequences that make the mechanism tangible
Evidence builds to the conclusion — the reader follows why the data leads to the specific conclusion drawn, rather than seeing evidence and argument as parallel tracks placed next to each other
Opposing positions or industry objections are characterized fairly and addressed on their strongest terms — the argument is firm without being adversarial or anti-business

The overall register conveys expertise through reasoning rather than rhetorical performance — summative sentences carry gravity without triumphalism, and the reader feels the weight of evidence, not the confidence of the advocate

rubric.name: Skimmability and Visual Structure
rubric.description: The document respects that not every reader will read start to finish. Section headers function as declarative signposts that carry the argument's conclusions — a reader who reads only the headers gets the argument's skeleton. Headers are direct and substantive, not neutral labels or formal legal titles.

dimensions.label: Section headers convey what each block is arguing or concluding — a reader who reads only headers can reconstruct the argument's skeleton
Headers read as direct, declarative signposts carrying argumentative substance, not as formal court-brief section titles or neutral topic labels
A reader who enters at any section can orient themselves without having read from the beginning
The relationship between sections is apparent from headers and opening sentences alone

coaching_notes: Non-negotiables: human stakes before institutional framing; no jargon without an immediate tether; tiered recommendations in every submission; headers that carry the argument. This user treats the sophisticated non-expert — the political appointee, the journalist, the advocate — as the benchmark reader, not the specialist. They consistently pull back from rhetorical performance and triumphalism, preferring authority earned through evidence and reasoning. When something isn't working, they diagnose structural problems with unusual precision — they'll identify a seam between evidence and conclusion, or a section doing double duty — and they respond best to fixes that preserve their voice rather than reimagine it.

rubric.name: Audience-First Framing
rubric.description: The writing leads with human experience and felt stakes, not institutional framing or procedural context. Regulatory and organizational context earns its place only after the reader already cares. Human stakes introduced early persist throughout the document — the reader never loses contact with the people affected, even in the most technical or legal sections.

dimensions.label: The opening leads with a human experience or felt problem, not institutional framing
The reader understands why they should care before being asked to follow the argument's architecture
The tone works for both expert readers and sophisticated non-experts without condescending
Regulatory, legal, or organizational context appears only after the reader has a reason to engage with it
Human stakes introduced early remain present or are reconnected in later sections — the affected person never fully disappears from the argument

rubric.name: Accessible Precision
rubric.description: Every technical, specialist, or insider term is immediately tethered to a concrete explanation — an example, a description of experience, or a plain-language clause that shows the reader what the term means in practice. The goal is to name it, then immediately show it, so the reader never has to translate on the fly. This is most critical at the moments where the text makes its key claims or asks. Legal citations and statutory references must convey the weight and relevance of their authority, not just the fact that authority exists.

dimensions.label: No jargon, term of art, or statutory citation appears without an immediate plain-language tether
Explanations use examples or descriptions of experience rather than formal definitions — the reader feels what the term describes before moving on
Reader understanding is most carefully supported at the moments where the text makes its key claims or requests
Technical concepts retain their substantive precision even after being made accessible — nothing is dumbed down
Legal precedents and statutory citations convey why the source matters and what it authorizes, not just that it exists — the reader feels the weight of the precedent

writing_type: public policy comments and regulatory submissions
user_goals_summary: The user writes public comments for federal regulatory proceedings that must persuade both expert staff and sophisticated non-specialist readers (political appointees, journalists, advocates). They value writing that leads with human stakes, grounds every claim in evidence, makes technical and legal language immediately comprehensible, and provides decision-makers with tiered recommendations specific enough to act on. Authority is earned through reasoning, not rhetorical performance.
coaching_notes: Non-negotiables: human stakes before institutional framing; no jargon without an immediate tether; tiered recommendations in every submission; headers that carry the argument. This user treats the sophisticated non-expert — the political appointee, the journalist, the advocate — as the benchmark reader, not the specialist. They consistently pull back from rhetorical performance and triumphalism, preferring authority earned through evidence and reasoning. When something isn't working, they diagnose structural problems with unusual precision — they'll identify a seam between evidence and conclusion, or a section doing double duty — and they respond best to fixes that preserve their voice rather than reimagine it.
